findings.leak_shape: When the SDK is constructed WITHOUT policies (the default '~614 baseline tests' path), `sdk.resolve(uri)` returns `resolved.object` raw for ALL five store types. For `cluster://ledger/<id>`, the consumer receives the raw ProvenanceEvent with `actorId` and `detail.payload` (which itself may contain the original command payload including Buffer-encoded artifact content). For `cluster://index/<id>` the raw IndexRecord with `metadata` (mirrors entity attributes). For `cluster://receipt/<id>` the raw Receipt with `resultSummary` containing entity names. Wave A3's SURFACE-R2-003 sanitization only fires inside the `if (this.policyEnforced)` arm.
findings.expected_protection: sanitizeProvenanceEventForOutput / sanitizeIndexRecordForOutput / sanitizeReceiptForOutput should run unconditionally before the SDK returns. The MCP's 'second pass at boundary' covers artifact+canonical but not the other three.

findings.leak_shape: The MCP `cluster_resolve` handler advertises itself as a 'second pass to harden the MCP boundary for callers that constructed the SDK without policies' (line 537-539) but only sanitizes `artifact` and `canonical`. `ledger`, `index`, and `receipt` store types fall through with `object = resolved.object` raw. Combined with V2-001: when MCP runs without DB_CLUSTER_POLICIES_FILE (the default), an MCP host calling `cluster_resolve` with `cluster://ledger/<event-id>` receives `actorId` and `detail` (command_payload) unredacted; with `cluster://index/<record-id>` receives metadata mirroring entity attributes; with `cluster://receipt/<id>` receives `resultSummary` with entity names embedded.
findings.expected_protection: Call sanitizeProvenanceEventForOutput / sanitizeIndexRecordForOutput / sanitizeReceiptForOutput for the three missing store types — symmetric to the policy-enforced SDK path at cluster-sdk.ts:239-244.

findings.leak_shape: Both `cluster_commit_mutation` (line 654) and `cluster_compensate_mutation` (line 671) return `result.receipt` raw across the MCP boundary. Wave A3 wired `sanitizeReceiptForOutput` into `cluster_list_receipts` (line 682) but missed these two sibling arms. policy-enforced-kernel.ts:511 itself states `resultSummary` contains entity names verbatim ('Created entity: User/john@example.com'); these tool surfaces leak that name back to any host that successfully commits or compensates — a permission they may have without owner-truth read rights.
findings.expected_protection: Wrap `result.receipt` with `sanitizeReceiptForOutput` in both arms, consistent with the `cluster_list_receipts` arm.

findings.leak_shape: The `ingest_artifact` arm of `commitMutation` still raw-casts `readyCommand.payload.content` to `Buffer`. src/integrations/repo-knowledge/ingest.ts:236-249 explicitly bypasses the lifecycle for this verb, noting that 'Buffer payloads do not round-trip through CommandQueue persistence (Buffer becomes `{type:'Buffer',data:number[]}` on rehydrate)'. The fix is documented as 'Stage B'. So a caller that DOES route an ingest_artifact through propose → validate → commit gets silent content corruption: `stores.artifact.ingest` writes a binary representation of the JSON-rehydrated object as content, computing a sha256 over the wrong bytes. Wave A3 left this kernel-internal boundary unfixed.
findings.expected_protection: The arm at line 527-552 should detect `content` shaped as `{type:'Buffer', data:number[]}` and rehydrate to `Buffer.from(payload.content.data)`. Without this, the JSON round-trip across CommandQueue persistence silently corrupts artifact content while still producing a 'successful' receipt — a worst case for data integrity.

findings.leak_shape: The CLI silently falls back to `INTERNAL_TRUSTED_PRINCIPAL` (cluster-admin) when `policies.json` is present but contains no `principal` field. The SDK was updated in Wave A3 (cluster-sdk.ts:159-162, SURFACE-R2-004) to `console.warn` loudly on this path — but the CLI substitutes the trusted principal at line 90 (and 632) BEFORE constructing the SDK. The SDK's `if (options.principal === undefined)` warn condition is never satisfied, so the warning never fires from CLI invocations. The CLI is the privileged operator surface; the Wave-A3 protection is fully bypassed there.
findings.expected_protection: Either (a) the CLI should emit an equivalent stderr warning before substituting INTERNAL_TRUSTED_PRINCIPAL, or (b) pass `principal: undefined` to the SDK so the SDK's warning fires.

findings.leak_shape: KERNEL-R2-001 'inspectCommand existence oracle' fix is partial. The first `enforce('read_command')` at line 488 runs WITHOUT `commandVerb`; then `await this.kernel.inspectCommand(commandId)` at line 492 — this throws NotFoundError if no such command exists. THEN the second `enforce` at line 493 with `commandVerb` runs and can throw PolicyDeniedError. A principal allowed to inspect commands generally but denied for a specific verb still observes a different error type for 'commandId exists but verb is denied' (PolicyDeniedError) versus 'commandId does not exist' (NotFoundError). That difference remains a usable existence oracle for verb-specific deny rules. Wave A3 only closed the case where `read_command` is denied at all.
findings.expected_protection: Catch NotFoundError from line 492 and re-throw as PolicyDeniedError when the principal has any verb-conditioned deny; or enumerate denied verbs per principal at session setup and reject the entire inspect surface up front.

findings.leak_shape: redactEntity and redactArtifact use a denylist approach that leaves leaky fields intact even on the most aggressive `strip` behavior. redactEntity('strip') keeps `id`, `kind`, `name`, `owner`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt` — only `attributes` is touched. If an entity's `name` is sensitive (e.g., 'User/john@example.com', or 'Patient Record #12345'), it leaks through redactEntity. redactArtifact('strip') keeps `id`, `filename`, `mimeType`, `contentHash`, `version`, `sizeBytes`, `ingestedAt`, `owner` — only `storagePath` is redacted. If filename embeds project codenames or PII, it leaks. The redactor is the LAST guard the wave relies on for entity/artifact data; its denylist scope means redactor-enforced policies leak name/filename by default.
findings.expected_protection: redactEntity('strip') should null/replace `name` (and arguably `kind`) when the rule targets entity_attributes. redactArtifact should similarly strip `filename` (mask to `${REDACTED}.${ext}` is fine — but only 'mask' behavior does this today). Switch to allowlist: only `id`, `owner`, and timestamps survive a 'strip' by default.
findings.evidence:
case 'strip':
    return { ...entity, attributes: {} };  // still leaks name, kind, id
...
case 'strip':
    return { ...redacted, storagePath: REDACTED };  // still leaks filename, mimeType, contentHash

findings.leak_shape: loadPolicyConfig() reads $CWD/.db-cluster/policies.json with NO structural validation of the `principal` field — unlike the MCP server which got the SURFACE-R005 `validatePrincipal` fix. A `policies.json` with `principal: { id: '', roles: 'not-an-array', trustZone: null }` is silently passed to PolicyEnforcedKernel. Concrete impact: malformed roles short-circuit `matchPrincipals` (policy-engine.ts:79 `[principal.id, ...principal.roles]` throws on non-iterable roles); empty `id` lets policy `match.principals: ['']` accidentally grant; unknown `trustZone` makes `applyTrustZoneRestrictions` (policy-engine.ts:137) skip the approval gate entirely. Wave A3 fixed this at the MCP boundary but missed the symmetric CLI boundary. The error message at line 71 also leaks `${POLICIES_FILE}` (absolute path) + raw err.message via stderr.
findings.expected_protection: Apply the same `validatePrincipal` from src/mcp/server.ts:43-52 in the CLI's loadPolicyConfig — fail loudly with a stderr message + process.exit(1).

findings.leak_shape: TraceBuilder embeds raw kind/name/actorId/subjectId into node labels and metadata. Line 104-108: entity nodes carry `label: '${entity.kind}: ${entity.name}'` and metadata `{kind, name, createdAt}`. Line 295-301: event nodes carry `label: '${event.action} by ${event.actorId}'` and metadata `{action, actorId, subjectId, subjectStore, timestamp}`. `redactGraphNodes` (policy-enforced-kernel.ts:407-411) only swaps a node's contents for '[Access restricted]' when `isNodeVisible` returns false — visible nodes pass through unchanged. `redactProvenanceActors` (redactor.ts:185-217) masks `actorId` in labels and a known set of metadata keys but does NOT touch `kind`, `name`, `subjectId`, or `resultSummary`. A principal allowed to trace_provenance but with an entity_attributes redaction rule still sees the entity's kind/name through the trace graph.
findings.expected_protection: When the trace-graph's matched policy carries an `entity_attributes` (or future `entity_identifiers`) redaction rule, redactGraphNodes / redactProvenanceActors should also rewrite `label` to omit kind/name and strip them from metadata.

findings.leak_shape: redactProvenanceActors masks actor identifiers via regex `by\s+[\w\-@.]+`. The character class misses common email/identifier chars: `+` (gmail sub-addressing — `user+test@x.com`), `!`, `$`, `&`, `'`, `(`, `)`, `:`, plus unicode. Worse, it ONLY catches `by <actor>` patterns — receipt-node labels at trace-builder.ts:328-332 are `Receipt: ${receipt.resultSummary}` where resultSummary contains entity-name leakage like 'Created entity: User/john@example.com'. The regex never fires on the entity-name portion. So a principal who has the `provenance_actors` redaction rule still sees full entity names through receipt node labels.
findings.expected_protection: Replace the regex with a more robust matcher (Unicode-aware, broader character class) AND extend redaction to receipt label entity-name patterns; or migrate to structured redaction where labels are reconstructed at render time from already-redacted node metadata.

findings.leak_shape: redactIndexSourceUri is exported from src/policy/index.ts (line 28) and declared as the canonical redactor for the `index_source_uri` rule target — but it is NEVER CALLED from anywhere in src/. `grep -rn redactIndexSourceUri src/` matches only the definition and the re-export. The policy type system advertises `index_source_uri` as a valid RedactionRule.target but no surface enforces it. A policy author who writes a rule with `target: 'index_source_uri'` will see their rule silently ignored. Index records returned through findSources / retrieveBundle leak `sourceId` + `sourceStore` to any caller with `read_derivative`.
findings.expected_protection: Either wire redactIndexSourceUri into PolicyEnforcedKernel.findSources / retrieveBundle index-record loops, or delete the function + remove `index_source_uri` from the RedactionTarget union so the type system reflects actual enforcement.

findings.leak_shape: PolicyDeniedError.message embeds `decision.matchedPolicyName` and `decision.reason` verbatim. MCP server.ts:786-790 catches errors and returns `{error: err.message}` to the host. So when policy denies a tool call, the host learns the exact name of the matched policy and its human-authored deny reason — both can be operationally sensitive (policy names may encode org structure; deny reasons may explain WHY: 'Customer PII restricted from external trust zone'). Wave A3 left this unchanged. Reaudit2 flagged similar shape for CorruptStoreError/CommandQueueCorruptError — but PolicyDeniedError carries the most sensitive policy-internal data and was not addressed.
findings.expected_protection: PolicyDeniedError.message should be a fixed string like 'Access denied'; decision details (policyName, reason) remain on the .decision property for internal logging but not in .message. The MCP error handler at server.ts:786-790 should additionally strip the .cause chain and limit error.message to a curated allowlist of error names.

findings.leak_shape: CLI `inspect <entity-id>` prints `JSON.stringify(entity.attributes)` directly to stdout. When the CLI runs under a PolicyEnforcedKernel, attributes WILL be redacted via the entity_attributes rule — but the CLI also prints `kind/name` raw (line 299-303) which redactor.ts:54-66 never touches (see V2-007). Similarly, CLI `trace --graph` (line 731-732) prints the full graph JSON including node labels and metadata. Operator-facing output via stdout is also captured in CI logs / shell history / piped consumers, so even 'operator-only' surfaces become information-flow leaks to anything that ingests CLI output.
findings.expected_protection: Either gate inspect/trace output through MCP-level sanitizers (sanitizeEntityForOutput is too weak — see V2-007) or require an explicit `--unsafe-raw` flag for full output. The surface currently presents as honoring policy redaction while leaking the most obvious identifiers.

findings.leak_shape: ClusterKernel.commitMutation records the FULL command payload (and verb + commandId) into the ledger via `recordProvenance(..., 'mutation_committed', ..., { commandId, verb, payload: readyCommand.payload })`. For an `ingest_artifact` mutation this means the `content: Buffer` of the artifact is persisted into the ledger detail — both as the orphan-recording metadata (line 641) AND the success-case provenance (line 614-621). Surfaces that later return provenance events (`traceProvenance`, `retrieveBundle.provenanceEvents`, `cluster_trace`) carry this raw payload through to the host. The `command_payload` redaction rule strips it through `redactProvenanceEvent` IF the caller's policy carries that rule, but the default (no rules) leaves the entire artifact content embedded in the ledger event detail, observable to any caller with `trace_provenance` or `read_derivative` on ledger.
findings.expected_protection: Either (a) record only a verb + commandId hash in the ledger detail, not the full payload; or (b) make the `command_payload` redaction rule unconditional for `mutation_committed`-action events. The current model conflates audit-essential identifiers (verb, commandId) with attacker-controlled content (payload).
findings.evidence:
provenance = await recordProvenance(
    this.stores.ledger,
    'mutation_committed',
    actorId,
    affectedIds[0] ?? commandId,
    readyCommand.targetStore,
    { commandId, verb: readyCommand.verb, payload: readyCommand.payload },  // payload is raw
);

findings.leak_shape: The index-record per-source policy filter (KERNEL-003) handles only `record.sourceStore === 'canonical'` and `record.sourceStore === 'artifact'`. The `else if (record.sourceStore === 'ledger')` branch is missing. ClusterKernel.explainIndex (cluster-kernel.ts:991-1001) demonstrates ledger sources are real — events can be indexed when `sourceStore === 'ledger'`. So a `findSources` response can include an index record pointing to a ledger event whose owner-truth visibility is never re-checked. The record passes through with full `text` + `metadata` to any caller holding `read_derivative` on index. The visibility veto at line 250 still applies but only if visibility rules cover ledger URIs — which the default rules do not.
findings.expected_protection: Add the symmetric `else if (record.sourceStore === 'ledger')` branch enforcing `read_owner_truth` (or its ledger-equivalent) for the underlying event. Match the pattern at line 233-246.

summary: The cross-boundary leak meta-pattern recurs throughout Wave A3, primarily at the SDK↔consumer and MCP↔host boundaries. Wave A3's headline fixes (SURFACE-R2-003 5-store-type sanitization, SURFACE-R2-002 symlink check, KERNEL-R2-001 inspectCommand oracle, KERNEL-R2-006 REDACTED sentinel) each closed their narrow target — but the same leak shape recurs nearby. SDK.resolve sanitization fires only under `this.policyEnforced` (V2-001); the MCP cluster_resolve secondary pass omits 3 of 5 store types (V2-002); cluster_commit_mutation/cluster_compensate_mutation skip the receipt sanitization that cluster_list_receipts got (V2-003); the inspectCommand oracle still leaks for verb-specific deny rules (V2-006); the CLI silently substitutes INTERNAL_TRUSTED_PRINCIPAL before the SDK's loud warning can fire (V2-005); the redactor's strip behavior is denylist not allowlist and leaks entity name/filename even with rules active (V2-007). The Buffer-JSON corruption bug from Wave A2 is still present in the kernel switch arm (V2-004) — Wave A3 added a workaround in repo-knowledge but didn't fix the root cause. V2-009/V2-010/V2-014 show the redactor/trace-builder pair has fundamental denylist gaps. V2-011 (redactIndexSourceUri dead code) and V2-015 (ledger source-store branch missing) are completeness gaps in the per-source policy filter.
